claim_layer: L2 structure only; no language named; no phonetic value used; no licence touched (Art. XV)

site_control_within_register.gate: every register pair's excess_ratio (max 1.2028) <= S=1.5117 -> ALL pairs site-discounted

LB_single_language_benchmark.meaning: one language (LB Greek), two document types, STILL systemic post-removal divergence xr=0.21 -> systemic phonotactic register divergence does NOT by itself indicate distinct languages

step3_vocabulary_attribution: NOT RUN for the record (gated: no pair survived the site gate); see posthoc
verdict_basis: PC1/PC2/PC3 all pass (machinery + power validated); all 3 LA register pairs Holm-significant at the permutation floor (p_holm=0.0015) BUT all are site-discounted: within-register site divergence (SEAL HT/nonHT excess_ratio 1.51) exceeds every between-register divergence (max 1.20) -> the register-vs-site attribution is undecidable on this corpus by the frozen rule

PH1_register_within_site_unconfounded.note: a real register contrast EXISTS when site is held fixed; LIB never co-occurs with LEDG/SEAL at a site, so libation-vs-admin is permanently site-confounded in this corpus

PH2_recurrent_word_removal_everywhere.reading: divergence is mostly recurrent-vocabulary; residuals (0.13-0.45) straddle the LB single-language benchmark (0.21); only LIB-involving residuals exceed it, and LIB is exactly the register that cannot be de-confounded from site

PH4_length_only_adversary.reading: word-length profile alone is significant only for LIB pairs (long formula words) and is tiny in absolute JSD; divergences are not length artifacts

bearing_on_F7_mixed_language: NO SUPPORT GAINED: (i) register divergence is not separable from site divergence at corpus scale; (ii) where site is held fixed (posthoc), the register residual after vocabulary removal (0.126) is BELOW the LB single-language ceiling (0.2125); (iii) LIB (libation) pairs exceed the ceiling but are permanently site-confounded. The mixed-language hypothesis remains unsupported at L2; nothing here refutes it either.
